perturbation.original_text: Vector hereby agrees that it and its Representatives shall use the Evaluation Material solely for the purpose of evaluating a possible transaction between the parties, that the Evaluation Material will be kept confidential and that Vector and its Representatives will not disclose any of the Evaluation Material in any manner whatsoever; provided, however, that (i) Vector may make any disclosure of such information to which Captaris gives its prior written consent and (ii) any of such information may be disclosed by Vector to its Representatives who need to know such information for the sole purpose of evaluating and implementing a possible transaction between the parties, who shall keep such information confidential and who are provided with a copy of this letter agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Vector hereby agrees that it and its Representatives shall use the Evaluation Material for the general purpose of evaluating a transaction between the parties, and that Vector and its Representatives will endeavor to keep the Evaluation Material confidential; provided, however, that (i) Vector may make any disclosure of such information to which Captaris gives its general consent and (ii) any of such information may be disclosed by Vector to its Representatives, who will try to keep such information confidential.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "solely for the purpose of evaluating a possible transaction" with "for the general purpose of evaluating a transaction" broadens the permitted use. Changing "will be kept confidential" to "will endeavor to keep the Evaluation Material confidential" weakens the obligation. Changing "gives its prior written consent" to "gives its general consent" and removing the clause "who shall keep such information confidential and who are provided with a copy of this letter agreement" from the clause on Representatives further undermines the confidentiality agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text weakens the confidentiality obligations, which conflicts with the CUTSA's protection of trade secrets. The CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, but the modified text's "endeavor to keep confidential" and "general consent" language falls short of the reasonable efforts standard, making it harder to enforce the agreement under California law.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the confidentiality obligations, potentially conflicting with trade secret protection laws. The original text requires Evaluation Material to be used 'solely for the purpose of evaluating a possible transaction' and 'will be kept confidential'. The changed text broadens the use to 'general purpose of evaluating a transaction' and weakens the confidentiality obligation to 'endeavor to keep the Evaluation Material confidential'. The law snippet defines 'improper means' as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By weakening the duty to maintain secrecy, the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation and makes it harder to enforce the agreement under trade secret laws. The original text's stronger confidentiality provisions align better with the legal duty to maintain secrecy, while the modified text's weaker language may not provide sufficient protection for trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: In addition, except as required by law or any rule of any self-regulatory organization, each of Captaris and Vector agrees that, without the prior written consent of the other party, it and its Representatives will not disclose to any other person the fact that the Evaluation Material has been exchanged, that discussions or negotiations are or may be taking place concerning a possible transaction involving the parties or any of the terms, conditions or other facts with respect thereto (including the status thereof) unless such first party has determined in good faith, after consultation with outside counsel, that such disclosure is required in order to avoid a violation of law or any rule of any self-regulatory organization.
perturbation.changed_text: In addition, each of Captaris and Vector generally agrees that, without the consent of the other party, it and its Representatives will try not to disclose to any other person the fact that the Evaluation Material may have been exchanged, that discussions or negotiations are or may be taking place concerning a possible transaction involving the parties or any of the terms, conditions or other facts with respect thereto (including the status thereof).
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "agrees" with "generally agrees" weakens the commitment. Changing "prior written consent" to "consent" removes the requirement for written consent, making it ambiguous. Changing "will not disclose" to "will try not to disclose" significantly weakens the non-disclosure obligation. Removing the clause requiring consultation with outside counsel and a determination in good faith further reduces the legal weight of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: The changes weaken the language around disclosure of a potential transaction, potentially increasing the risk of insider trading. While the original text had safeguards (written consent, legal consultation), the modified version lacks these protections. This increased risk can violate Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5, which prohibit the use of manipulative or deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities, including the misuse of material non-public information.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 2B / § 78j Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC 78j : Manipulative and deceptive devices Text contains those laws in effect on July 12, 2025 From Title 15-COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 2B-SECURITIES EXCHANGES Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Effective Date Regulations §78j. Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the non-disclosure obligation concerning a potential transaction, increasing the risk of insider trading and potentially violating Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. The original text had safeguards like 'prior written consent' and 'consultation with outside counsel,' whereas the modified version weakens these protections by changing 'agrees' to 'generally agrees,' 'prior written consent' to 'consent,' and 'will not disclose' to 'will try not to disclose.' This introduces ambiguity and reduces the enforceability of the non-disclosure agreement. The removal of the consultation requirement further exacerbates this issue. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 15 USC 78j, which pertains to manipulative and deceptive devices, providing a basis for the Law Explanation's concern about violations of securities laws related to non-public information.

perturbation.original_text: If either party decides that it does not wish to proceed with a transaction, it will promptly inform the other party of that decision. In that case, or any time upon the request of a party for any reason, Vector will promptly, at Vector’s option (i) return to Captaris and/or (ii) destroy all Evaluation Material (and all tangible copies thereof) furnished to it or its Representatives by or on behalf of the other party pursuant hereto, and in either case no copies thereof shall be retained except that, any of the foregoing notwithstanding, that portion of the Evaluation Material that may be found in analyses, compilations, studies, or other documents prepared by Vector’s Representatives, such Representatives’ agents, attorneys or employees, and oral or electronic Evaluation Material will be held by such Representatives and kept subject to the terms of this agreement or destroyed to the extent practicable and permitted by law.
perturbation.changed_text: If either party decides that it does not wish to proceed with a transaction, it should inform the other party of that decision. In that case, or any time upon the request of a party, Vector may return or destroy the Evaluation Material. Any Evaluation Material that may be found in analyses, compilations, studies, or other documents prepared by Vector’s Representatives, agents, attorneys or employees, and oral or electronic Evaluation Material will be held by such Representatives and kept confidential to the extent possible.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "it will promptly inform" to "it should inform" weakens the obligation to notify. Replacing "Vector will promptly...return to Captaris and/or destroy" with "Vector may return or destroy" makes the return/destruction of Evaluation Material optional. Removing "at Vector's option" creates ambiguity. Changing "subject to the terms of this agreement or destroyed to the extent practicable and permitted by law" to "kept confidential to the extent possible" further weakens the confidentiality obligation and its enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: The weakened language concerning the return or destruction of confidential information, especially when tied to potential litigation, can create issues under California discovery rules. Specifically, if a party only *tries* to keep information confidential *to the extent possible,* a court could find that they failed to reasonably protect it. This could then lead to sanctions under CCP 2031.310 for failing to properly respond to discovery requests, particularly regarding the destruction of documents. While the agreement concerns a transaction, not necessarily pending litigation, weakening these protections invites future discovery disputes and potential liability should litigation arise.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 2031.310 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 4. CIVIL DISCOVERY ACT [2016.010 - 2036.050] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) CHAPTER 14. Inspection, Copying, Testing, Sampling, and Production of Documents, Electronically Stored Information, Tangible Things, Land, and Other Property [2031.010 - 2031.510] ( Heading of Chapter 14 amended by Stats. 2012, Ch. 72, Sec. 30. ) ARTICLE 2. Response to Inspection Demand [2031.210 - 2031.320] ( Article 2 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) 2031.310. (a) On receipt of a response to a demand for inspection, copying, testing, or sampling, the demanding party may move for an order compelling further response to the demand if the demanding party deems that any of the following apply: (1) A statement of compliance with the demand is incomplete. (2) A representation of inability to comply is inadequate, incomplete, or evasive. (3) An objection in the response is without merit or too general. (b) A motion under subdivision (a) shall comply with each of the following: (1) The motion shall set forth specific facts showing good cause justifying the discovery sought by the demand. (2) The motion shall be accompanied by a meet and confer
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates specific actions regarding confidential information. The changed text weakens these obligations, potentially creating a contradiction with discovery rules. Specifically, CCP 2031.310(a) allows a party to compel further response to a demand for inspection if a statement of compliance is incomplete. The changed contract's language, making confidentiality protection 'to the extent possible,' introduces ambiguity. This could be interpreted as an incomplete effort to protect confidential information, opening the door to motions to compel under CCP 2031.310(a). The original text provided stronger assurance of compliance, which is now undermined.

perturbation.original_text: In consideration of the Evaluation Material being furnished by Captaris hereunder, Vector hereby agrees that, for a period of twelve months from the date hereof, it will not, and will not encourage or assist its affiliates to, solicit for employment (a) any officers or other senior key employees of Captaris or any of its business units so long as they are employed by the Captaris or its business units, without obtaining the prior written consent of Captaris, and (b) any of the other employees of Captaris or any of its business units with whom Vector has had contact or who Vector first became aware of during the period of its investigation of Captaris, so long as they are employed by the Captaris or its business units, without obtaining the prior written consent of Captaris.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of the Evaluation Material being furnished by Captaris hereunder, Vector agrees that, for a period of approximately twelve months from the date hereof, it will try not to solicit for employment (a) any officers or other senior key employees of Captaris or its business units, and (b) other employees of Captaris or its business units with whom Vector has had contact or who Vector first became aware of during the period of its investigation of Captaris.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "hereby agrees that, for a period of twelve months" to "agrees that, for a period of approximately twelve months" introduces ambiguity to the duration of the agreement. Changing "will not, and will not encourage or assist its affiliates to, solicit" to "will try not to solicit" weakens the non-solicitation obligation. Removing the requirement for "prior written consent of Captaris" eliminates a clear enforcement mechanism.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While there are exceptions, such as for the sale of a business, the modified text, which weakens the non-solicitation clause, makes it more likely that a court would deem the clause unenforceable under Section 16600 because the lack of a definite time and obligation (making it 'approximately twelve months' and only requiring Vector to 'try not to solicit') suggests that the restraint is broader than necessary to protect a legitimate business interest. This ambiguity could lead a court to invalidate the clause as an illegal restraint of trade under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the non-solicitation obligation, making it potentially unenforceable under California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. The original text had a definite period ('twelve months') and a firm commitment ('will not solicit'). The changed text uses 'approximately twelve months' and 'will try not to solicit,' removing the original clarity and enforceability. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 16600(a) states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' The modifications weaken the restraint, increasing the likelihood a court would find it unenforceable under this section.
